title: Introducing Apex
intro: Apex is a strongly typed, object-oriented programming language that executes flow and transaction control statements on the Salesforce Platform server. It uses Java-like syntax and behaves like database stored procedures — letting you add business logic to system events, button clicks, Visualforce pages, and web service requests.

points.text: All variables and expressions have a data type, checked at compile time. Invalid references fail fast.
Runs entirely on Salesforce's multitenant Lightning Platform — written, compiled, and executed in the cloud.
Familiar for developers who know Java, JavaScript, C#, or similar languages.
Supports Savepoints and rollback — you can control the atomic unit of work.
DML, SOQL, SOSL, locking, custom public API calls, and metadata dependency tracking are all native.
DML, inline SOQL/SOSL, bulk processing, locking, public API calls, and dependency warnings are built in.
Based on familiar Java idioms — variables, conditionals, loops, objects, arrays.
Threads multiple query and DML statements into one unit of work, like database stored procedures.
Direct references to schema objects. Fails at compile time if references are invalid.
Interpreted, executed, and controlled entirely by the Lightning Platform.
Runtime engine guards against runaway code to prevent monopolizing shared resources.
Built-in support for unit test creation, execution, and code coverage.
You can save Apex against different API versions to maintain behavior over time.

sections.heading: 1.3 When to Use Apex (vs Flow / LWC / Visualforce)

points.text: You need to create web services, email services, complex multi-object validation, complex business processes Flow can't handle, custom transactional logic, or logic that must run regardless of UI origin.
For custom user interfaces in Lightning Experience, the Salesforce mobile app, or standalone apps. LWC follows W3C web standards.
For wizards, multi-step processes, custom flow control, or specialized data-driven UI patterns.
When you only process one type of record at a time and don't need transactional control.
Render UI elements other than error messages.
Change standard functionality — it can only prevent it or add to it.
Create temporary files.
Spawn threads.
It is NOT a general-purpose programming language — it is tailored to Salesforce data access and manipulation.

sections.heading: 1.5 The Apex Development Process
sections.intro: The recommended 6-step lifecycle for any Apex project:

points.text: Sandbox, scratch org, or Developer Edition. Never develop directly in production.
Use Trailhead, Apex Developer Center, and this study guide.
Use VS Code + Salesforce Extensions, Code Builder, or Developer Console.
Use test-driven development — write tests at the same time as code.
Run final unit tests in an isolated environment.
Via change sets, Salesforce CLI, or Metadata API.

sections.heading: 1.6 Choosing a Salesforce Org for Development

points.text: Copy of production metadata, isolated. Developer and Developer Pro sandboxes support source tracking, Salesforce CLI, Code Builder, and DevOps Center.
Source-driven, temporary, fully configurable deployment. Max 30-day lifespan, default 7 days. Great for emulating different editions.
Free org with Enterprise Edition features. Can become outdated and expire. No source tracking, can't be used in DevOps Center.
30-day expiration. Good for evaluation. Triggers are disabled when you convert to another edition.
Never develop directly in production — live users can destabilize data or corrupt the application.
The primary modern environment. Supports scratch orgs, sandboxes, and DE orgs. Works with Apex, LWC, and Visualforce.
Browser-based VS Code with everything pre-installed — work from any computer.
Built-in IDE in Salesforce. Supports writing, compiling, debugging, testing, SOQL queries, and code coverage inspection.
View and edit Apex classes and triggers from Salesforce Setup. Auto-compile on save. Not available in production orgs.
AI-powered code generation from natural language prompts, inline auto-complete, and test case generation. Available in VS Code.

title: Data Types and Variables
intro: Apex uses data types, variables, enums, constants, expressions, operators, and assignment statements. Type checking is strictly enforced at compile time — assigning a String to an Integer field is a compile error.

points.text: Integer, Double, Long, Decimal, Boolean, String, ID, Date, Datetime, Time, Blob, Object.
Generic sObject or specific types like Account, Contact, MyCustomObject__c.
Lists, Sets, and Maps of primitives, sObjects, or user-defined types.
A typed list of identifiers for values that don't have a numerical order.
Objects created from your own Apex classes.
Objects from Salesforce-provided classes like Database, UserInfo, Schema.
Can be assigned to any variable. All Apex variables are initialized to null by default.

sections.heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
sections.intro: All Apex variables are initialized to null. Always initialize before use.

points.text: Binary data stored as a single object. Use toString() and valueOf() to convert. Used for document bodies, attachments, and crypto.
true, false, or null. Example: Boolean isWinner = true;
A particular day with no time. Always create via system static method. Only Integer addition/subtraction works on Date.
A day and time (timestamp). Use system static methods. Supports Integer and Double addition/subtraction.
Arbitrary precision number. Currency fields are Decimal. Use setScale() to control decimal places. Two numerically-equal Decimals with different scale (1.1 vs 1.10) have different hashcodes.
64-bit number with decimal point. Range is -2^63 to 2^63-1. Scientific notation (e) not supported.
Any valid 18-character Salesforce record ID. Auto-converts 15-char to 18-char. Invalid IDs throw runtime exception.
32-bit number without decimal. Range: -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647.
64-bit number without decimal. Range: -2^63 to 2^63-1. Use when wider range than Integer is needed. Append L: 2147483648L.
Any supported Apex data type. All types inherit from Object. Cast to a specific type to access its members.
Any set of characters in single quotes. Supports ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=. Comparison is NOT case-sensitive and uses the context user's locale.
A particular time. Always create via system static method.
\b (backspace), \t (tab), \n (line feed), \f (form feed), \r (carriage return), \s (space), \" (double quote), \' (single quote), \\ (backslash).
sObject String fields can never be empty (only null) and cannot have leading/trailing whitespace — required for database storage.
Apex Strings CAN be null, empty, or have leading/trailing whitespace — useful for building messages.
Starts with three single quotes (''') followed by a newline. Ends with three single quotes. Leading whitespace is stripped, trailing whitespace is stripped unless you use \s.
Use \ at the end of a line to concatenate lines without inserting newlines.

sections.heading: 2.4 Lists
sections.intro: A list is an ordered collection of elements distinguished by their indices. The first index is always 0.

points.text: Use the new keyword and List keyword with the element type in <>.
points.code: List<Integer> myList = new List<Integer>();

points.text: Use the .add() method, or use literal notation with braces.
points.code: List<String> colors = new List<String>{'Red', 'Green', 'Blue'};

points.text: Use .get(index) and .set(index, value). Square brackets also work: colors[0] = 'Green'.
For one-dimensional lists, you can use Java-style array notation: String[] colors = new String[3];
Up to 7 levels of nested collections (8 levels overall). Example: List<List<Set<Integer>>>.
Sorts in ascending order for primitives. For custom types, implement Comparable. For SelectOption, sorts by value first, then label.
When you add via .add(), the list grows as needed. When you assign via square brackets, the list behaves like a fixed-size array.

sections.heading: 2.5 Sets
sections.intro: A set is an unordered collection of unique elements.

points.text: Adding an existing element has no effect. Highly optimized for .contains() checks.
You cannot access a set element by index. You can only iterate.
Unlike Java, Apex uses a hash structure for all sets. You don't specify HashSet vs TreeSet.
Set<String> s = new Set<String>{'a', 'b', 'c'};
points.code: Set<String> mySet = new Set<String>{'New York', 'Paris'};

points.text: .add(value), .contains(value), .remove(value), .size(), .clear().

sections.heading: 2.6 Maps
sections.intro: A map is a collection of key-value pairs where each unique key maps to a single value.

points.text: Can be any primitive data type. Each key must be unique.
Can be any data type — primitives, sObjects, collections. Values can be duplicated.
Use => to separate key and value pairs.
points.code: Map<Integer, String> m = new Map<Integer, String>{1 => 'a', 2 => 'b'};

points.text: .put(key, value), .get(key), .containsKey(key), .keySet(), .values(), .remove(key), .size().
An abstract data type whose values each take on exactly one of a finite set of identifiers you specify.
For sets of possible values that don't have a numerical order — e.g., seasons, card suits, order statuses.
Enums work especially well with switch statements in Apex.
datatype variable_name [= value]; — always end statements with a semicolon.
Integer, String, etc. are passed into methods by value. Changes inside the method are lost when the method returns.
sObjects are passed by reference. Changes to field values persist after the method returns.
Use the final keyword. Once initialized, the value cannot change.
points.code: private static final Integer MAX_RECORDS = 200;

sections.heading: 2.9 Operators and Expressions
sections.intro: Apex supports most Java operators plus two important modern ones: safe navigation (?.) and null coalescing (??).

points.text: Returns null instead of throwing NullPointerException when navigating a chain. Example: account?.Contact?.Email.
points.code: String email = acc?.Contact?.Email; // null if acc or Contact is null

points.text: Returns the right-hand operand when the left-hand is null. Replaces verbose null checks.
points.code:
Integer x = maybeNull ?? 100;
Account a = [SELECT Id FROM Account WHERE Id=:fakeId] ?? defaultAccount;

points.text: Apex Strings support ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=. NOT case-sensitive, uses context user's locale.
boolean ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse
Check if an sObject is a specific type: if (sobj instanceof Account) { ... }
1. Grouping and prefix: {} () ++ --
2. Unary, type cast, new: ~ ! -x +x (type) new
3. Multiplication/division: * /
4. Addition/subtraction: + -
6. Comparison: < <= > >= instanceof
7. Equality: == !=
8-10. Bitwise: & ^ |
13. Null coalescing: ??
14. Ternary: ?:
Hierarchy: Integer → Long → Double → Decimal. Lower types can be assigned to higher types without explicit conversion.
IDs can be assigned to Strings. Strings can be assigned to IDs but throw runtime exception if invalid.
Both implicit and explicit casting work. Time component is zeroed out.
Apex does NOT throw an exception for overflow/underflow — Integer i = 2147483647 + 1; yields -2147483648. Use Long (append L) for larger computations.
5/3 returns 1 (Integer) before implicit conversion. Use 5.0/3.0 to preserve fractions.

title: Control Flow Statements
intro: Apex provides if-else statements, switch statements, and five types of loops. Statements normally execute line by line; control flow lets you branch or repeat.

points.text: Works just like Java. The else is always optional and groups with the closest if.
Chain multiple conditions. else is always optional.
Curly braces are required for multi-statement blocks, optional for single-statement blocks.

sections.heading: 3.2 Switch Statements (Modern Apex)
sections.intro: Apex switch uses when blocks — no fall-through.

points.text: switch on expression { when value { ... } when else { ... } }
After a when block executes, the switch exits. This is different from C/Java switch.
Integer, Long, sObject, String, Enum.
A when clause can have multiple comma-separated literals: when 2, 3, 4 { ... }
switch on sobject { when Account a { ... } when Contact c { ... } } — implicit instanceof + cast.
Optional default block. Salesforce recommends including it, especially with enums (in case a managed package adds new values).

sections.heading: 3.3 The Five Loop Types
sections.intro: Apex supports five procedural loop patterns. All loops support break; and continue;.

points.text: Runs the body first, then checks the condition. Always executes at least once. Curly braces are required.
Checks the condition before the body. May execute zero times.
Three-part: initialization; exit condition; increment.
Iterates a list or set. Enhanced for-each loop.
Iterates query results directly. Best for bulk processing — heap-efficient.
Exits the entire loop immediately.
Skips the rest of the current iteration and proceeds to the next.

title: Classes, Objects, and Interfaces
intro: Apex classes are blueprints for objects. A class can contain variables, methods, properties, constructors, and initialization code. Use access modifiers to control visibility.

points.text: public class MyClass { ... }
If you omit a modifier, the class is private (only visible within the same namespace).
Visible to any Apex in the application or namespace.
Only visible within the same class. Not accessible from other classes.
Visible to all Apex code everywhere, including code in other namespaces. Required for exposed web services and managed package APIs.
Class cannot be instantiated. Used as a base class. Can contain abstract methods (no body).
Class can be extended. Use virtual when subclasses need to override methods.
Enforces the running user's sharing rules and record-level access.
Bypasses sharing rules. Use carefully — typically for system-level utilities.
Inherits sharing context from the calling code. Available in API v48.0+.
Belongs to the class, not an instance. Accessed via ClassName.method(). Only one copy exists.
Belongs to an object. Requires an instance to call. Each object has its own copy.
Static variables are initialized when the class is first loaded. Use static { } block for static initialization code.
Cannot access instance variables or methods. Often used for utility/helper methods.
public, private, protected, global — control visibility.
Static methods belong to the class; instance methods require an object.
Specify a return type (Integer, String, void, sObject, etc.) or void for no return.
Apex supports method overloading — multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists.
Primitives pass by value. sObjects pass by reference (field changes persist).
Initialize an object when it is created via new.
If you don't write a constructor, Apex provides a no-argument constructor automatically.
You can write multiple constructors with different parameter lists.
Constructors don't have a return type, not even void.
Define a contract — methods that implementing classes must provide. Apex supports interfaces and abstract classes.
Interface methods have no body. Implementing class must provide the body.
A class can implement multiple interfaces (use commas), but can only extend one class.
Database.Batchable<SObject>, Schedulable, Queueable, Comparable, Iterator, Iterable.
A property looks like a field but is actually a pair of get/set accessors. Cleaner than explicit getter/setter methods.
public Integer Count { get; set; } — Apex auto-generates the backing field and accessors.
You can provide custom getter and setter logic blocks.
A class can extend exactly one parent class. Use 'virtual' on the parent to allow overriding.
Use the override keyword to override a virtual or abstract method from a parent.
Methods marked virtual can be overridden. Abstract methods MUST be overridden.
Call the parent implementation with super.methodName().

title: Working with sObjects and DML
section: Chapter 5: Working with sObjects and DML
intro: The sObject data type holds Salesforce records. Data Manipulation Language (DML) operations insert, update, delete, undelete, merge, and convert records.

points.text: Any data type that represents a Salesforce record — standard (Account, Contact) or custom (MyObject__c).
sObject generic type can hold any record. Use specific types (Account, Contact) whenever possible for type safety.
Always end with __c. Custom fields end with __c.
Use the new keyword and the sObject type name. Pass field values as named parameters.
points.code:
Account a = new Account(Name='Acme', BillingCity='SF');
insert a;

sections.heading: 5.2 DML Statements
sections.intro: Six core DML operations. All run in the current transaction context.

points.text: Adds new records. Sets Id automatically.
Modifies existing records. Must have Id populated.
Updates if record with matching external/Id field exists; otherwise inserts. Requires an external ID field or Id.
Removes records. Goes to Recycle Bin for 15 days; can be undeleted within that window.
Restores records from the Recycle Bin.
Combines up to 3 records of the same sObject type into one. Merges child records too.
Direct DML — runs 'all or nothing'. If one record fails, the entire transaction rolls back.
Database.insert(records, allOrNone). Use false to allow partial success.
points.code:
Database.insert(accs, false); // partial success allowed
Database.update(conts, true); // all or nothing

points.text: When you need partial-success behavior or to inspect individual record errors. Returns Database.SaveResult[] or Database.DeleteResult[].
Marks a point in the transaction you can roll back to.
Only 10 active savepoints per request. Each rollback decrements the count.
Try a DML operation, roll back if it fails, and continue with logic.
Locks sObject records during the transaction to prevent race conditions.
points.code: Account[] accs = [SELECT Id FROM Account WHERE Industry = 'Tech' FOR UPDATE];

points.text: When subsequent code will update the records and you need to prevent other transactions from modifying them.
Traverse a lookup using dot notation: account.Owner.Name.
Query through a relationship: SELECT Account.Owner.Name FROM Contact.
For custom __r: SELECT My_Custom_Child__r.Name FROM Account.

title: SOQL and SOSL
intro: Two query languages for retrieving data. SOQL for relational, structured queries against a single object. SOSL for free-text search across multiple objects simultaneously.

points.text: Salesforce Object Query Language. Query relational data from a single object or related objects.
SELECT field1, field2 FROM ObjectName WHERE condition
points.code: List<Account> accs = [SELECT Id, Name FROM Account WHERE Industry = 'Technology'];

points.text: Don't use SELECT * — always list fields explicitly for performance and clarity.
SELECT Id, Account.Name FROM Contact
SELECT Id, (SELECT Id, LastName FROM Contacts) FROM Account
Filter records. Use bind variables for user input: WHERE Name = :varName
Sort results: ORDER BY Name ASC, CreatedDate DESC
Cap returned records: LIMIT 100
Skip records for pagination: OFFSET 200
Aggregate. SELECT COUNT(Id), Industry FROM Account GROUP BY Industry HAVING COUNT(Id) > 5
Lock records: SELECT Id FROM Account WHERE Name = 'Acme' FOR UPDATE
Used in trigger context variables to get old values without locking.
Enable tracking for change data capture events.
Enforces field-level security (FLS) and sharing rules. Recommended over WITH SECURITY_ENFORCED.
points.code: List<Account> accs = [SELECT Id, Name FROM Account WITH USER_MODE];

points.text: Bypasses FLS and sharing. Use for system code only (e.g., setup operations).
points.code: [SELECT Id FROM Account WITH SYSTEM_MODE]

points.text: Use UserMode/AccessLevel enums: WITH USER_MODE or [SELECT Id FROM Account AS USER] or [AS SYSTEM].
Returns Integer. SELECT COUNT(Id) FROM Account
Numeric aggregates. SELECT MAX(Amount), MIN(Amount) FROM Opportunity
Required for most aggregates. SELECT Industry, COUNT(Id) cnt FROM Account GROUP BY Industry
Find the relationship name via object manager. For Contacts on Account, it's 'Contacts' (plural).
Ends with __r for the parent reference. SELECT My_Child__c FROM Parent__c
WHERE Account.Industry = 'Tech' — filter using a related object's field.
Salesforce Object Search Language. Free-text search across multiple, unrelated objects at once.
FIND 'searchTerm' IN ALL FIELDS RETURNING Object(field1, field2)
points.code:
List<List<SObject>> results = [FIND 'Acme' IN ALL FIELDS
  RETURNING Account(Id, Name), Contact(Id, FirstName, LastName)];

points.text: List<List<SObject>> — a list of result lists, one per object type.
When you don't know which object has the data, or need to search many objects at once. Faster for free-text than SOQL on large text fields.
Build SOQL as a String at runtime. Use bind variables to prevent SOQL injection.
points.code:
String s = 'SELECT Id FROM Account WHERE Industry = :ind';
List<SObject> rows = Database.query(s);

points.text: Same idea for SOSL. Returns List<List<SObject>>.
Use String.escapeSingleQuotes() and Database.query with bind variables to prevent injection.

title: Triggers
intro: Triggers are Apex code that execute before or after records of a particular type are inserted, updated, deleted, or undeleted. All triggers run in bulk — they process multiple records at once.

points.text: trigger TriggerName on ObjectName (events) { ... }
before insert, before update, before delete, after insert, after update, after delete, after undelete.
A single trigger can handle multiple events. Branch with Trigger.operationType.

sections.heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
sections.intro: Implicitly defined in every trigger. Provide access to the records and event context.

points.text: List of new sObject versions. Available in all events. Read-only in after triggers.
List of old sObject versions. Available in update, delete, undelete.
Map<Id, sObject> of new versions, keyed by Id. Available in update, insert, delete, undelete (post-save).
Map<Id, sObject> of old versions, keyed by Id. Available in update, delete.
Boolean. True if the trigger fired on insert.
Booleans for the current context.
Number of records in the trigger invocation — useful for bulk handling.
Enum: BEFORE_INSERT, BEFORE_UPDATE, BEFORE_DELETE, AFTER_INSERT, AFTER_UPDATE, AFTER_DELETE, AFTER_UNDELETE.

sections.heading: 7.3 Trigger Events and What They Allow

points.text: Modify field values on Trigger.new directly. No DML needed. Use for validation and data normalization.
Trigger.new is read-only. Use for related record creation and other side effects. DML allowed.
Use Trigger.old to inspect. Can call addError() to block deletion.
Use Trigger.old or Trigger.new. Good for cleanup or restoration logic.
Never put SOQL or DML inside a for loop. Collect criteria into Sets/Lists, query once, iterate results.
Use a single trigger per object with a handler class to keep logic organized.
Use a static Boolean flag or Set<Id> to prevent recursive firing from cascading updates.
Best practice: put logic in a handler class, keep the trigger a one-liner that calls it.

title: Governor Limits and Bulkification
intro: Because Apex runs on a shared multitenant platform, strict governor limits prevent any one execution from monopolizing resources. All Apex is bounded — you must design for bulk.

points.text: 100 queries per synchronous transaction. 200 in async (Batch/Future/Queueable/Schedulable).
50,000 rows retrieved in a single transaction.
150 statements per synchronous transaction.
10,000 records processed per transaction.
6 MB synchronous, 12 MB asynchronous.
10,000 ms synchronous, 60,000 ms asynchronous.
100 HTTP callouts per transaction.
100 sendEmail methods per transaction.
50 per transaction.
1 per transaction (50 with TransactionFinalizers in chain).

sections.heading: 8.2 Bulkification
sections.intro: The single most important Apex design pattern. Handle many records as efficiently as one.

points.text: Code that processes a List of 200 records with the same number of SOQL/DML as 1 record.
for (Account a : Trigger.new) { List<Contact> cs = [SELECT Id FROM Contact WHERE AccountId = :a.Id]; } — hits SOQL limit fast.
Collect all AccountIds into a Set, run ONE SOQL, iterate.
Build lists of records to update/insert, then perform ONE DML on the whole list at the end of the trigger.

sections.heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
sections.intro: When a record is saved, Salesforce runs through a specific order. Knowing this helps debug and design.

points.text: 1. System validation rules (required fields, field format)
2. Before triggers run
3. Custom validation rules
4. Record saved to database (but not committed)
5. After triggers run
6. Assignment rules
7. Auto-response rules
8. Workflow rules
9. Processes (Flows, Process Builder — being retired)
10. Escalation rules
11. Entitlement rules
12. Roll-up summary fields update child records
13. Criteria-based sharing evaluation
14. DML commits to database
All Apex code runs on shared, multitenant infrastructure.
Limits prevent one tenant's runaway code from degrading the platform for everyone.
All Apex requests return 1 to 50,000 records — you must design for bulk.
If you don't, you'll hit governor limits in production with real data.

title: Exception Handling
intro: Apex uses try-catch-finally for exception handling. Some built-in exceptions (DmlException, QueryException, NullPointerException) cover the most common failure modes.

points.text: try { ... } catch (ExceptionType e) { ... } finally { ... }
Always runs, even if an exception is thrown. Use for cleanup (Database.rollback, logging).
Thrown when a DML operation fails. Use getDmlMessage(i) and getDmlId(i) to inspect each error.
Thrown when a SOQL query fails — invalid SOQL, query assigned to single sObject but returns 0 or many rows.
Thrown when dereferencing a null variable. Use safe navigation (?. ) to avoid.
Thrown on invalid list operations — index out of bounds, modifying list during for-each.
Thrown when a governor limit is exceeded.
Thrown on arithmetic errors like division by zero.
Extend the Exception class. Optionally pass a message to the constructor.
points.code: public class MyException extends Exception {}

points.text: throw new MyException('Something went wrong');
catch (MyException e) { System.debug(e.getMessage()); }
In a trigger, call e.addError('msg') to roll back the transaction and display the error to the user.
acc.Name.addError('Name is required'); — error attached to a specific field.
Use addError() in triggers for user-facing validation. Use try-catch for programmatic error handling.

title: Testing Apex
intro: Testing is critical — Salesforce requires 75% code coverage before deploying Apex, but the real goal is verifying every use case works. Use test-driven development.

points.text: Mark the class and methods as test code. @IsTest classes don't count against the 6 MB Apex code limit.
Test methods must be static, return void, and take no arguments.
Equivalent to @IsTest. Salesforce recommends @IsTest — testMethod may be versioned out.
At least 75% of Apex code must be covered by tests. All tests must pass. Every trigger must have some coverage.
Create test data once for the entire class. Re-runs before each test method to ensure isolation.
Annotate a method with @TestSetup. It must be static.
Use [SELECT Id FROM Account LIMIT 1] to fetch the records created by @TestSetup.
Apex sets a new governor limit boundary inside startTest/stopTest. Use to simulate large data and isolate async code.
Wrap the code under test between startTest and stopTest. After stopTest, async code has completed.
Future methods, Batch Apex, and Queueable jobs only execute inside a startTest/stopTest block.
assert(condition) — fails if false.
assertEquals(expected, actual) — fails if not equal.
assertNotEquals(unexpected, actual)
Assert class with areEqual, areNotEqual, isTrue, isFalse, isNull, isNotNull.
One logical assertion per test. Test one thing at a time.
By default, tests cannot see pre-existing org data. They only see data they create.
Test can see existing org data. Avoid — makes tests fragile and dependent on org state.
Always create your own test data. Don't depend on org data existing.
Insert sObjects in your test, then re-query and verify field values reflect trigger logic.
Insert a list of 200+ records to verify your trigger handles bulk correctly.
Test before insert, before update, after insert, after update — every branch of your trigger.

title: Asynchronous Apex
intro: When you need to break out of synchronous limits — long operations, large data volumes, scheduled logic, or chaining — use asynchronous Apex. Four flavors cover most needs.

points.text: A simple way to run Apex asynchronously. Mark a static method with @future.
Future methods must be static, return void, and accept only primitive or collection of primitive parameters.
50 future calls per transaction. Async limits are higher than sync (200 SOQL, 12 MB heap, 60s CPU).
Make a web service callout, do work that shouldn't block the user, fire-and-forget logic.
points.code:
@future(callout=true)
public static void makeCallout(Id accId) { ... }

points.text: A more powerful async option that supports non-primitive types and job chaining.
Implement Queueable and the execute(QueueableContext) method.
Call System.enqueueJob() at the end of execute() to chain jobs together.
1 queueable job per transaction (50 with chaining via TransactionFinalizers).
Process millions of records in chunks. Each chunk runs in its own transaction with its own governor limits.
Implement Database.Batchable<SObject> with three methods: start, execute, finish.
Implement to maintain state (instance variables) across execute() calls. Default is stateless.
Up to 2000 per chunk (default 200). Larger chunks may hit heap limits.
Run Apex at a specific time. Cron-like scheduling.
Implement Schedulable and the execute(SchedulableContext) method.
Cron syntax: 'Seconds Minutes Hours DayOfMonth Month DayOfWeek Year'. Use System.schedule().

title: ApexDoc and Code Documentation
intro: ApexDoc is a standardized comment format, based on JavaDoc, that makes your Apex code easier for humans, documentation generators, and AI agents to understand. Use it for collaboration and long-term maintainability.

points.text: /** Description followed by tags. */ placed immediately above classes, methods, or properties.
Describes a method parameter. One per parameter.
Describes what the method returns.
Documents the exception types a method can throw.
Additional descriptive tags.
AI agents and code-completion tools use doc comments to understand intent.
Future developers (including future you) will thank you.
Auto-generated documentation tools can produce reference docs from your comments.
ApexDoc tags are required by Salesforce for managed packages and AppExchange apps.
